channel.title: Comments for page "The Not-Too-Distant Future"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Not-Too-Distant Future"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You're very welcome! I'm happy to provide a peek behind the curtain — I've written a lot of esoteric, experimental stuff into this story, and some of it is buried real deep. I always like providing some insight on how it all came together.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glorious, thank you. And a second thanks for the detailed author post.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fair enough! I like to control the perceived tone of the story, but I can lose the forest for the trees. I've pared it back a bit. Thanks for the feedback, and the compliment - I really enjoy writing dialogue, particularly between these characters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Taking a break from reading to say the italics are really overdone throughout, and the italics that are underlined (and the ones that are bold) feel excessive. They lose usefulness as an emphasis tool if they're that frequent, and I'm fairly sure I would have internally emphasized a lot of the italicized words without any special formatting. It feels distracting.

Dialogue's great so far though. Much more natural than most tales.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"In the good mystery there is nothing wasted, no sentence, no word that is not significant. And even if it is not significant, it has the potential to be so - which amounts to the same thing. The world of the book comes to life, seething with possibilities, with secrets and contradictions."

- Paul Auster, "City of Glass", 1985

"Pure referentialism is boring. […] There needs to be a central framework, a consistent thematic structure to make it all go. Otherwise, it's just… throwing shit out and seeing what sticks. lolrandom. Childish. Weak."

- [EXPUNGED], July 23 2019

Wake up in an unfamiliar place, and you may tell yourself… "this is not my beautiful house! How did I get here?" Finding answers can be dangerous. Some patterns resent being observed.

Broadly speaking, this series — which I'm calling The External MxTape — is about owning, coping with and learning from mistakes. No easy feat, given that all our misdeeds are tangled up in a complicated web of social responsibilities. We can try to rectify things through honest and open dialogue, but that choice can take lots of time and preparation. Some folks have an easier time processing trauma when it's filtered through a cultural layer, and that is where steakshift comes in.

Wren Masterson is a reality bender (no matter how they spin it), but that doesn't make them real. In fact, Masterson has an extremely difficult time believing in themselves, because perception is not reality, and on a universal scale… well, is anything real at all? They try to keep their head down and abide by a simple quid pro quo, but MxMasters' life is too disordered, contrived and surreal to be governed by actual logic. Instead, their world is ruled by narrative inconvenience, and they have very big debts to pay off… not necessarily in linear order. Penelope Gore sees things like a normal human, but from Wren Masterson's perspective, this whole cataclysmic chain of events is about to play out in reverse.

Thanks go out to kinchtheknifeblade for taking an early look at the concept, and to cybersqyd for detailed critique! Much appreciated!

Easter Eggs
Easter Eggs
  • The call/response sequence is patterned on "Telephone", by Lady Gaga and Beyoncé. It started as a callback to Penelope getting trolled with Gaga in SCP-4581, but as it turns out… the music video for "Telephone" is a Tarantino homage! Synchronicity. Funny how that works sometimes.
  • "Eat it, Rorschach!" GAW is fundamentally a pacifist group, but some people truly deserve to get Manhattan'd… if that Agent Smith, Daffy Duck motherfucker ever existed at all. (Pakoosh!)
  • Episodes 420 and 421 of MST3K are "The Human Duplicators" and "Monster A-Go-Go". Like "Telephone", I originally picked these as a simple joke ("lol 420 for GAW"), but they've ended up having deeper thematic resonance than expected. We'll circle back to them eventually…
  • The number 23 is meaningless. Don't think about it too hard. Safer that way.
